Until about 6 months ago it was level-pegging the general index, but since then it has outperformed it by a significant margin. It has done this on several occasions in the past decade (e.g., 2019, 2020, and 2022-3), only to fall back in line in time.

I first covered the company in September—doing something of a deep-dive – giving it a buy rating at the time. This proved prescient, with the stock up 20% since then, significantly outpacing the S&P 500, which is down 1%.

One of the key reasons for this company’s consistent performance is its wide range of products/treatments sold, which helps derisk and diversify its business. It also allows it to take operational risks by pursuing growth in new areas. Indeed, while they have pursued some different treatment areas in the past (including antiviral therapies), presently they have four main foci: respiratory inflammation and autoimmunity; cardiovascular and metabolic diseases; oncology; and, after their acquisition of Alexion Pharmaceuticals in 2021, rare diseases. In 2025, oncology was their largest and fastest-growing segment, representing 44% of product revenue (as seen below) and seeing 14% revenue growth across the full franchise. Also seen below, the US represents their largest market, with 43% of revenue, but with R&D centres in Cambridge (UK), Gaithersburg (US), Boston (US), Gothenburg (Sweden), Shanghai (China), and Beijing (China), they can establish a presence and sell/market their products on a truly global scale.

Highlighting its diversification in terms of product revenue, in 2025, the company had 12 drugs that generated over a billion dollars in revenue.

It also had another 5 drugs between $300 million and a billion in 2025 revenue. They had 16 drugs reach “blockbuster” status, meaning over $500 million in annual revenue, and recently noted their objective was to have more than 25 by 2030.

Alongside its broad drug spectrum and track record of innovation is its growing dividend since inception, representing another key reason for solid share-price performance. With a current 2% yield and several decades of dividend growth—underpinned and supported by its strong FCF generation—shareholders remain well-rewarded here. Indeed, even if the company continues to level-peg the general index as it has done for a decade or so, shareholders could handily beat the index when factoring in the dividend yield, especially if it is reinvested to buy more shares/partial shares.

Given its recent outperformance over the past few months, what investors would surely like to know is whether this may be the start of a breakout for the company. Or perhaps it is likely to fall back in line with its historical performance, relatively in step with the general index. In this article, I’ll assess the major news pieces and developments since my first write-up, consider new growth drivers, and provide an analysis of its financials and valuation metrics to give a full picture of its risk/reward profile.

Recent Developments

AstraZeneca reported full-year 2025 financial results on Feb 10. They reported 8% revenue growth and 11% EPS growth year-over-year.

Given how broad and extensive the company’s products are, it is a challenge to parse out any specific projects/products that may impact future revenue because there are so many of them. While this may prevent them from ever growing beyond the low double-digit marker annually, they are also well protected through patent/exclusivity losses or competitive threats. Indeed, given they are governed by the law of large numbers, perhaps the best way to determine future growth rates is to consider growth in these large numbers.

Current Product Franchise

The company’s short-term revenue drivers remain their core products already in the market—notably their top 4: Farxiga, Tagrisso, Imfinzi, and Ultomiris—which together generate $26.44 billion, or 47.58% of their $55.57 billion product revenue in 2025. Their top 6 products (adding Calquence and Lynparza), which each generated over $3 billion in revenue in 2025, represented just shy of 60% of their total product revenue. Of these, Imfinzi grew the most in 2025, delivering 29% year over year. Ultomiris grew 20%, while Calquence grew 12%. Tagrisso and Farxiga both grew 10% since 2024, while Lynparza increased revenue by 7%.

Farxiga, their best-selling drug in 2025 for diabetes management, chronic kidney disease, and heart failure, saw 10% revenue growth, but there are anticipated struggles ahead for the drug due to its patent protection in the US ending in March 2028, resulting in a loss of exclusivity. In China, Farxiga (and Lynparza) are due to be included in the volume-based procurement (VBP) program in the first half of 2026. The VBP program was introduced in 2018 as an avenue to lower drug prices for patients through volume-based trade-offs, with most drugs being included here seeing around 50% price reductions. Management is expecting strong growth in Europe and ex-China to partially offset these anticipated declines, but the speed of revenue decline as we look into 2026 and 2027 is unclear at this point. Given the drug represented around 8% of total product revenue in 2025, the results certainly won’t be disastrous, but neither will they be immaterial. According to AstraZeneca’s own publication, Farxiga faces patent expiration in Europe in 2027; their main patents have already expired in China and Japan, but some extensions cover them in these two countries until 2028. The writing is on the wall for this one, as generics are entering the market, so efforts by the company to diversify away from this blockbuster have been welcomed.

Tagrisso was AstraZeneca’s second best-selling drug in 2025 and their leading oncology drug. It is approved to treat EGFR-mutated non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), which represents between 16% and 19% of NSCLC conditions. According to this research paper from 2023, Tagrisso has become the standard of care here. It has at least another 6 years of patent protection and should continue its strong performance going forward, but I note that growth slowed from 13% from 2023-24 to 10% from 2024-25. The drug is currently being trialed in several different studies in NSCLC, with readouts expected in late 2026 and 2027.

The strong growth of their second leading oncology drug, Imfinzi, was attributed, according to management, to new launch indications in bladder cancer and lung cancer. This was offset slightly by mandatory price reductions in Japan that commenced in early Feb 2024 (by 25%) and were extended a further 11% in Aug 2024, alongside increased competition in biliary tract cancer. Growth was strongest in the US and emerging markets. At this growth rate, Imfinzi is set to become the company’s best-selling drug—overtaking Tagrisso—by 2027. AstraZeneca is expecting two key readouts to come in 1H 2026. Both are combination therapies with Imjudo. The first targets Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC), which is the most common type of liver cancer, most typically arising in patients with underlying cirrhosis or chronic hepatitis. The second extends their treatment of bladder cancer.

Ultomiris, their treatment for four different rare diseases, continued its strong revenue growth in 2025. With another nine years of patent protection in their leading markets, the drug should continue performing well. It has effectively replaced Soliris, its previous iteration, across the same approved indications, due primarily to its lower patient burden and longer-acting performance; patients were required to take Soliris every two weeks, but with Ultomiris, it is every eight weeks. Revenue growth for Ultomiris in emerging markets has been particularly strong, increasing by 84%, but the US (56.5% of 2025 revenue) and Europe (22.3%) represent their largest markets.

AstraZeneca’s recent results also highlight strong double-digit growth rates across five other drugs that are set to have a material difference to their top-line performance should growth rates continue. Of those generating a billion in revenue for the year, the two leading growth drivers were their asthma drug Tezspire (65%)—jointly owned with Amgen (AMGN)—and oncology treatment Enhurtu (40%), which is jointly owned with Daiichi Sankyo (DSNKY). Also performing well were Breztri (23% growth), Strensiq (19%), and Fasenra (17%).

Portfolio Growth and Innovation

AstraZeneca has shown themselves to be excellent investors in their own business, mainly through R&D. The table below shows that as revenue has increased, so too has the proportion of R&D expenses. R&D has increased as a proportion of SG&A, which has also declined as a proportion of generated revenue.

Their strong FCF generation has allowed them to invest more back into future growth. What impresses me most about this company is its consistent long-term focus. They are always looking 5-10 years ahead so they can continue to develop their pipeline as their existing drug program reaches maturity, before it declines due to patent expiration. This has allowed it to sustain its position as a leading biopharmaceutical company over the decades, and this gives me confidence that it should sustain its position here over time. It produces a kind of flywheel effect: R&D leads to more products, which drives revenue growth; as they manage their general SG&A expenses but increase the proportion of R&D within these expenses, their efficiency increases, so they can then invest more in what is driving long-term business growth. This has led to sustained increases over the last 5 years in returns on equity, assets, and invested capital (shades of orange/red below) and operating, net profit, and FCF margins (shades of green below).

Gross margins were not included in the chart above for aesthetic purposes but have also improved marginally over the past 5 years, as seen below. The company is clearly being well managed and oriented for long-term performance.

Their expansive pipeline remains incredibly impressive. As of Feb 10, they have 197 projects in their pipeline, 125 new molecular entities (NMEs) or major life-cycle management in phase 2 or 3 trials, 20 NMEs in the late-stage pipeline, and 4 NMEs under review. AstraZeneca currently has over a hundred phase 3 research studies ongoing and is expected to generate >20 phase 3 readouts in the year. Presently, they are expecting near-term catalysts with potential FDA approval for their blood pressure drug Baxdrostat, which is under review, and management expressed excitement for Tozorakimab, their COPD lung disease drug, after recent positive trial data.

The company has a stellar track record of innovation for shareholders to fall back on when assessing investment risk and reward.

So, when assessing their diverse offerings in both current products and pipeline, the figures on the far-right side of the chart above paint a picture of growth. This, to me, is their most important chart featured in their Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings presentation, which the CEO Pascal Soriot, described as follows:

‘Importantly, you can see that there's a growing number of late-stage assets, but importantly, an increasing value per indication. As our pipeline grows, we continue to focus and prioritize. And of course, we prioritize the most valuable projects. And you can see in light pink the average peak-year revenue per indication. That reflects the increasing individual value of projects and the continuous effort we make to prioritize even though we have a lot of projects.’

We can see in this chart above that the number of unique assets in phase 3 trials has been stable at 37, showing no increase from 2024. Also, the anticipated PYR (peak year revenue) figure has increased but slowed somewhat to just over $1,200. This means the total and average annual revenue from their total franchise of drugs/treatment has increased and should continue to do so over the following years. This figure will fluctuate minimally as revenue fluctuates due to competition, patents, new indications, approval extensions, and a host of other reasons, but over the long term these fluctuations are smoothed out, showing long-term sustained growth. For me, the company has evidently built a strong moat around their business and a flywheel for sustained long-term performance. When you also factor in their strong growth in emerging markets (22% outside of China), which should slowly but meaningfully increase their TAM across their entire portfolio, the future for AstraZeneca looks bright. Secular tailwinds are driving some of this growth, but the company is clearly executing well on its long-term growth strategy through strategic investments and collaborations.

AstraZeneca reports earnings for Q1 2026 on 29th April. At the end of 2025, they guided for total revenue growth of mid/high single digits in 2026 and EPS growth of low double digits for the year. These are shown below. They also reiterated their $80 billion revenue expectation by 2030. This would represent a 6.64% CAGR, which is very achievable given their strong sales track record, patent and exclusivity protections, and expanding pipeline. I expect them to hit both short- and long-term targets, if not slightly exceed them.

With improving operating and net margins, this 6.64% CAGR could translate to high single-digit net profit/earnings growth over the next 5 years. With stock buybacks, this could translate to low single-digit EPS growth (as they have forecasted for 2026), and with dividend growth, shareholders could achieve 12-15% returns on their investment annually, which would continue to track slightly ahead of the S&P 500 benchmark, which has stood at 11% or so over recent decades.

Let’s now assess the company’s financials and valuation metrics to gauge risk/reward given the recent share-price performance.

Financials and Valuation Metrics

AstraZeneca has cash and equivalents of $5.74 billion amidst current assets valued at $28.7 billion. Goodwill, valued at $21.2 billion, is a fairly large chunk of their total assets ($114 billion), but this is not a concern given they have managed to improve their returns on assets, equity, and invested capital over the years, thereby proving their capital allocation strategy. They have current liabilities of $30.6 billion, which is above their current assets, giving them a current ratio below 1. This would ordinarily worry me slightly, but given that so much of their revenue is predictable—and that they generate so much FCF per year ($11.77 billion in 2025)—I am not particularly concerned here. Theoretically, they could use their FCF to pay off 100% of their total debt (valued at $29.6 billion) within 3 years. The current portion of this total debt is just $3.1 billion. I note that the company has sustained a net debt per share over the last few years, and this has stayed consistent between $7 and $8. The company’s book value has increased over time, so shareholder have not really been punished with their large debt load. Indeed, they have evidently used debt very well to invest in growth through R&D.

In terms of valuation, AstraZeneca now trades at a trailing PE of 31 according to Seeking Alpha, and a forward PE (non-GAAP) of 39. Other publications give its forward PE closer to 20, which in any case is still above the pharmaceutical average of around 17.7 (according to LSEG Reports) and also slightly above their own 5-year average.

The charts above measure its figures against the UK’s FTSE index, as they very recently discontinued their ADR stock listing to secure a full listing on the NYSE. This means equivalent charts on the NYSE only stretch back a few months, so they are less useful to gauge long-term valuation trends. In any event, in terms of valuation, the chart below shows its PEG (non-GAAP) increasing above what I would consider a buy range for this stock. Seeking Alpha has it even higher at 2.5.

Risks and Concluding Thoughts

Seeking Alpha’s valuation grade of F hints at the same problem: a slightly stretched valuation, perhaps caused by strong buying as investors pursue a flight to safety in the current macroeconomic climate. Of course, this could continue, but I would not be surprised if a better entry point comes to light in the next 6-12 months, as the company’s fundamentals catch up to the higher valuation that AZN shares now trade at with their most recent upswing. While much of AstraZeneca's premium value is tied to its historically very strong track record of success, some of their future success is already priced in.

This means that investors have pushed up the price of AZN shares and the PE multiples, based at least somewhat on their enduring trust in management. Of course, the company must execute, and any signs of either increasing competitive threats (especially in oncology, their leading area) or harsher than expected revenue declines due to patent cliffs and cheaper generics being launched, could shift the investment narrative here. For some drugs, the impacts of this were seen in 2025. Last year, their cardiovascular, renal, and metabolic treatment area saw revenue growth dip to just 2%. One of their leading medicines for reducing the rate of cardiovascular death, myocardial infarction, or stroke in patients with acute coronary syndrome (Brilinta), saw year-over-year revenue decline 38% from 2024-25; from over $1.3 billion to $823 million. This was due to patent expiry in the US and Europe for their lead indications. Generics began entering the market in 2025, which not only stole market share but also forced AstraZeneca to lower the price of Brilinta in order to compete. In this particular case, as in others, the revenue decline was offset by revenue increases in oncology and for other specific drugs, which is why we must look long-term at general trends and competitive threats to assess risk in biopharma players.

There are also revenue trends in China to keep an eye on, as management mentioned slower growth in this country in previous earnings calls. They remain an enormous market, and any new additions to their VBP program could have an outsized impact on AstraZeneca's revenues. For reference, revenue from China for the full year was $5.15 billion, representing 11% of the total.

Overall, I'd argue that AstraZeneca remains one of the safer biopharma stocks given its large product portfolio, a strong pipeline that can withstand patent cliffs on lead products, alongside its broad exposure to durable secular trends in its key treatment areas (e.g., oncology, rare diseases). However, the threat of competition always remains, and investors should pay close attention to their leading drugs in this regard, as market share losses by large percentages could have a high weighted impact.

I continue to rate AstraZeneca a long-term buy. While I see the company is continuing to progress with its pipeline, I do not necessarily see a major inflection point ahead. I predict slight market outperformance eventually but a “reversion to the mean” in its near-term future, as valuations fall back in line with their historical averages. I do not believe the company deserves to trade at such a high premium given that its projected growth rates in both revenue and earnings remain solid but not spectacular.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: My Plan To Wait It Out Fell Apart
data.description:
Over the past several years, a major theme in commercial real estate has been to sit it out and wait. Following the pandemic, trouble in the commercial mortgage sector gave birth to the phrase extend and pretend. The idea was that troubles at the asset level that were caused by rising interest rates could be solved by simply ignoring fundamentals and refinancing loans. Much of the trouble that banks faced with commercial real estate was navigated by this strategy.

Looking more broadly, the strategy of waiting out a troubled market has become a core theme in real estate. If we are to look at the office sector, it continues to lead the way as a struggling asset class. If we are to look at more niche assets, such as life science, we see that an even more dreadful pattern has emerged.

Today, we will revisit Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) and discuss why the “wait it out” strategy is falling apart.

Review of Prior Coverage

ARE is one of the most storied businesses in real estate. The company virtually built the life science real estate business and for decades was one of the highest performers in real estate. For the ten years following the Great Recession, ARE did well, but post-pandemic, the company ran circles around commercial real estate more broadly.

The company had all of the pieces necessary for a successful business. The company had a cost of capital advantage, the life science industry was booming, and skeptics were beginning to turn their opinion on whether life science was a viable business. The pandemic supercharged their business, and ARE reached for the skies.

That is until everything came crashing down.

Several years after the pandemic, a combination of a supply-demand shift in life science and rising interest rates led to one of the most catastrophic periods in the history of life science real estate. This led to what many have been calling the five-year bear market in biotech.

I began covering ARE as the pieces began to fall. At the end of 2024, I initiated coverage, posting an article with a Strong Buy that overviewed the company and the business model. Subsequently, I have published additional articles covering individual assets that the company owns in San Diego as well as substantial leasing activity.

In my coverage of the company, my thesis wrapped around the idea that the company was going to wait out the trouble in the biotech market. The company had a relatively low payout ratio and a low debt load, which made the balance sheet seem prepared to withstand continued low demand in life science real estate.

This all changed when it became clear that ARE was not in a position to wait out the bear market. The company had an incredibly large development pipeline with enormous capital commitments driving straight into continued lower demand. This meant that the company was spending billions of dollars on real estate development to build assets that it would have trouble finding a tenant to occupy. Over the past year, occupancy has continued to tick lower, and the company continues to hover around the dreaded 90% occupancy mark.

Looking at their own chart begs the question of why the company has been waiting until recently to take their foot off the development gas pedal.

Some of the trouble that the company ran into was to be expected, but some of the negative developments around the funding of medical and life science research were an unexpected turn for the worse for the company's business model. Combined with ongoing troubles and the realization that they had overcommitted on development spending, ARE elected to slash the dividend substantially and begin withdrawing certain development plans.

In the most recent investor presentation, ARE mentioned their forward development spend was around 40% smaller than the post-pandemic peak, a reduction of $1.1 billion per year. More assets are being moved to held for sale, and fewer office conversions are taking place.

This led to ARE posting some dreadful estimates for earnings this year and forward. Leverage metrics are beginning to deteriorate, undoing much of the improvement in the post-financial crisis era.

Let’s just say my plan to hop into ARE in early 2025 and wait out the turmoil hasn’t gone particularly well.

Past performance is not always indicative of future returns; let’s explore the market outlook and risk factors for ARE.

Market Update

A key piece of the puzzle for ARE is whether or not the life science market is poised for a comeback. As shown above, demand across the United States has remained low for life science, with funding constraints and a supply overload pressuring owners.

A recent research report from Colliers mentions that the wave of supply is finally being digested. With over 50 million square feet delivered over the past five years, national supply increased by around 30% in a short period. Development has declined significantly, and there are only around seven million square feet currently under construction.

Entering 2026, only about seven million square feet remains under construction, a significant portion of which is either build to suit or has some level of preleasing in place. Market vacancies, tenant demand, construction costs, asset pricing, interest rates, and lending standards have all shifted against developers over the past few years. As a result, speculative groundbreakings have been, and should remain, very limited across most markets for the foreseeable future. In some cases, developers are selling sites that had been slated for lab projects or are revising plans in favor of other property types

Colliers also mentions the continued trend of sales and repurposing away from life science. ARE’s recent investor report is consistent with this trend as they continue to try and trim away noncore assets in the portfolio.

Meanwhile, Cushman & Wakefield paints an encouraging picture of the biotech market, mentioning that funding seems to be making a comeback.

Capital markets also showed signs of life. Global R&D investment sales climbed to $13.5 billion, up 28% year-over-year, with the U.S. leading the rebound. VC funding held steady at $49 billion, supported by strong momentum in APAC, and Europe is beginning to see early improvements in debt markets, M&A activity, and public sector innovation funding.

Cushman & Wakefield also mentions that vacancy has continued to rise, reaching 23.1% in early 2026.

Right now, the market continues to look weak for ARE. The company’s most important markets, like San Diego, remain under pressure. As an example, San Diego’s vacancy rate remains above 25%, according to Cushman & Wakefield.

While we can continue to sit on our hands and wait out the storm, the question is whether or not ARE will be able to do so.

Risks

In the most recent investor presentation, ARE posted some metrics that are pretty tough. While the company has been trimming noncore assets to fund debt repayments, buybacks, and near-term development spending, the loss of revenue has been noticeable. Right now, occupancy has been declining, and the portfolio is shrinking. This puts per-share earnings under pressure.

Ok, “under pressure” was kind. Lower occupancy and poor disposition performance took to earnings like a 12-gauge shotgun, and FFO per share is projected to decline by almost 30% year over year. This marks the most significant negative earnings shift since the Great Recession. Peak to trouble, ARE’s share performance is similar as well.

The company introduced forward guidance that shows FFO per share stabilizing in the second half of the year as the company readjusts spending plans and completed dispositions. Based on their forward estimates for 2027, a starting point for FFO per share is around $6 per share.

This chart does a great job of summarizing operating performance for ARE.

The company also has a considerable amount of debt to be refinanced in the coming years. ARE should be able to retain enough cash, sell enough assets, and keep their investment-grade rating in place, which should help navigate. Still, the company’s weighted average interest rate of 3.97% is well below where ARE has recently issued new bonds.

Currently, virtually every operating metric for ARE is negative. The company is hoping to stabilize operations at the end of this year and then once again rebuild their platform and earnings. The issue is that the markets still do not appear ready to embark on the same trend. This means the ARE could be dead in the water until there is a significant turnaround in the biotech space.

Conclusion

What on earth do you do with a company like ARE? What was once a blue chip now looks like a black sheep as shareholder value craters. Life science has all but dried up, and the current administration does not seem fond of using government funds to begin catalyzing a return to science.

This likely leaves ARE dead in the water for the foreseeable future. Those who decide to hold onto the “wait it out” approach, like me, have found themselves holding a rather light bag. Right now, shares look cheap for a reason. Without a compelling catalyst in place, ARE earns a Hold rating.

data.title: SCHD's 2026 Reconstitution: Nature Is Healing
data.description:
The Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is a cornerstone of my dividend growth portfolio.

I'm sure the same is true for a great many dividend investors.

This passively managed and ultra-cheap ETF is one of the most well-structured and balanced dividend funds on the market, combining a moderate yield with robust dividend growth.

When I last wrote about SCHD (for High Yield Investor) late last year, I argued that "SCHD's Dividend Growth Formula Is Broken (For Now)." Indeed, 2025 saw the second-lowest year of dividend growth in SCHD's history.

The reason for that underperformance and slowed dividend growth pertains to the 2024 and 2025 reconstitutions. In the March 2024 reconstitution, the ETF sold some early AI winners like Broadcom (AVGO), which went on to double in price over the next few years. And in the March 2025 reconstitution, SCHD stuffed its portfolio with oil companies, pushing the energy sector exposure to more than 20%.

Right after that March 2025 reconstitution, President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement caused the market to drop and led to the underperformance of the energy and consumer staples sectors (SCHD's two largest sectoral weightings at the time) for the rest of the year.

This year, however, the spike in oil prices has been a godsend for SCHD, allowing the ETF to sell appreciated oil companies and reinvest into attractively valued dividend growth stocks in other sectors.

Nature is healing.

In my view, SCHD is buyable again.

Let's dive in to explore why.

A Brief History of SCHD

Lots of dividend ETFs sport yields over 3%. That's not unusual.

And plenty of dividend ETFs have exhibited strong dividend growth in the high single digits over time, although they tend to have low yields in the 1-2% area.

What's unique about SCHD is the fact that it offers a dividend yield well north of 3% (currently 3.4%) as well as a robust dividend growth record.

Since inception, SCHD's cumulative dividend growth has meaningfully outrun that of peers like the First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders Fund (FDL), iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), and iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV):

As you can see, SCHD's incredible dividend growth run continued right up through Q1 2024, after which it began to stall.

I think that's because the broad-based bull market from 2012 through 2023 gave way to an increasingly concentrated, AI-driven market in 2024 and 2025.

Historically, maybe half of SCHD's dividend growth came from the dividend growth of its underlying holdings, while the other (roughly) half came from portfolio recycling -- selling appreciated lower-yielders and reinvesting into depreciated higher-yielders.

During a broad-based bull market, that strategy works well because SCHD's portfolio has plenty of winners to cull in order to reallocate funds to more attractive dividend stocks.

But when a large enough portion of SCHD's portfolio has not appreciated much, reconstitutions turn out to be a wash, generating no yield uplift.

Broad-based bull markets are also great for SCHD in terms of total returns (price appreciation + dividends).

Here's the period from 2012 through 2021, comparing SCHD and DGRO to the S&P 500 (SPY):

Yes, SPY still beat SCHD, but not by a large margin. SCHD spent much of this almost decade-long period roughly matching SPY's performance.

Then came 2022, the year of the post-COVID hangover for high-growth and speculative parts of the market. The tech-heavy SPY underperformed, giving SCHD's defensive tilt an opportunity to shine.

For more conservative investors (including myself), 2022 showcased why SCHD makes such a great foundation for a dividend growth portfolio.

I imagine many investors who own SCHD are willing to give up some upside during bull markets for less downside during bear markets.

But then came the three-year raging bull market of 2023-2025, and SCHD went back to underperformance.

And when I say "underperformance," I mean severe underperformance.

Even aside from the unfortunate aspects of the 2024 and 2025 reconstitutions, SCHD simply wasn't built to thrive in an increasingly top-heavy and concentrated bull market like what we saw during this period. But the reconstitutions added to the pain.

That brings us to this year, in which two narratives have dominated so far:

  1. In January and February, the market systematically shifted away from AI and industries (like software) that plausibly face disruption by AI while piling into previously overlooked value and defensive stocks. SCHD did very well in this environment.
  2. From March to the present, the Iran war and oil prices have dominated the news, wreaking havoc among almost all sectors besides energy.

Right toward the end of the chart above, SCHD underwent its 2026 reconstitution.

So let's now look at that.

Details About the March 2026 Reconstitution

In my view, it would be wise not to read the 2026 reconstitution in light of the current Middle East conflict and over-$100 oil prices. The ETF wasn't designed to double down on the narrow slice of the market that works well during times like this, and I'm glad it wasn't.

Take a look at this chart of forward P/E ratios between the SPY (red line) and the energy sector (XLE):

The energy sector now trades at almost the same valuation as the tech-heavy SPY. That's extremely rare.

Ignore the two big spikes in 2016 and 2020, because the price of oil was in deep dips during those periods, causing deeply depressed earnings for energy companies.

Even with energy companies becoming more disciplined and returning more cash to shareholders, I think it makes more sense right now to trim one's energy holdings rather than buy more.

That is exactly what SCHD did in its 2026 reconstitution.

Here's a summary of the sector shifts:

Healthcare and materials up, energy and materials down.

The stock-picking algorithm did exactly what it was supposed to do: Sell high and buy low.

The main way SCHD accomplished that was by trimming some of its larger holdings that surpassed the individual stock limit while outright selling a number of others.

Here's a detailed breakdown of the additions and deletions:

(Click on the image to enlarge it on your screen.)

Most of these trades have very little bearing on the performance of the fund due to their tiny weightings, so let's focus only on the top five buys and sells.

I'm very pleased to see such high-quality compounders as UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Abbott Labs (ABT), Procter & Gamble (PG), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Accenture (ACN) gracing SCHD's portfolio, especially when bought on a dip like this.

UNH currently yields about 3.2%, while ABT yields ~2.5%, PG yields almost 3%, QCOM yields 2.8%, and ACN yields 3.2%.

These are great dividend yields for such high-quality dividend growth stocks!

And on the other side of the ledger, four of the top five holding sales have ripped mightily over the last year:

Oil companies Valero (VLO) and Halliburton (HAL), as well as the fertilizer company CF Industries (CF), look like well-timed and opportunistic dispositions.

The only head-scratcher here is AbbVie (ABBV), which was already in a slight dip when SCHD sold. I won't pretend to understand why SCHD exited ABBV, but a consolation prize is that the ETF's overall healthcare exposure increased.

I see the preceding portfolio recycling as a prudent and well-timed reformulation of the fund, making it more balanced and resilient.

I really wanted to see SCHD reduce its heavy exposure to the energy sector, and I got my wish.

In the process, as usual, SCHD buffed some of its quality metrics, including small increases in free cash flow to debt, return on equity, and past dividend growth:

The only very slight sacrifice was the fund's overall dividend yield, which slipped 2 basis points.

As of early April, the top holdings list is still topped by two energy giants, which is to be expected given their strong performance recently.

But overall, I consider SCHD's top holdings as a solid mix of blue-chip dividend growers.

And for better or worse, SCHD is and will remain an overwhelmingly large-cap ETF. The only mid-cap holdings (largely in the financial sector) command very small weightings.

Bottom Line

So far (knock on wood), this year's reconstitution of SCHD's portfolio looks like the mirror opposite of last year's.

Whereas the March 2025 reconstitution saw SCHD pile into energy stocks at exactly the wrong time, it looks like the 2026 reconstitution had SCHD significantly trim its energy holdings at exactly the right time.

Even beyond the energy piece, this year's reconstitution has renewed my optimism about this moderate-yielding yet high-quality dividend growth ETF.

According to Morningstar data, SCHD's forward P/E ratio now sits at about 15.3x, well below the SPY's ~20x multiple.

The ETF yields 3.4%, and I believe high-single-digit dividend growth is plausible going forward.

In the world of dividend ETFs, you arguably won't find much better than SCHD.
